coredata.dc:title: Ultra-sensitive aptasensor based on a GQD nanocomposite for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen
coredata.dc:creator: Ghanbari, Kazhal
Roushani, Mahmoud
Azadbakht, Azadeh
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, by using the aptamer proximity binding assay strategy, a novel electrochemical aptasensor is described for ultrasensitive detection of hepatitis C virus (HCV) core antigen. The immobilization surface is prepared by the modification of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a graphene quantum dots (GQD). GQD were introduced as a novel and suitable substrate for aptamers through π-π stacking interactions, the richness of hydrophilic edges as well as hydrophobic plane in GQD which enhances the aptamer absorption on the electrode surface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed at each stage of the chemical modification process to confirm the resulting surface changes. EIS technique was used as an efficient alternative detection system for HCV core antigen measurement with detection limit 3.3 pg mL−1 and two linear concentration range 10–70 pg mL−1 and 70–400 pg mL−1. Moreover, the fabricated aptasensor could accurately detect HCV core antigen concentration in human serum samples. Such an aptasensor opens a rapid, selective and sensitive route for HCV core antigen detection and provides a promising strategy for potential applications in clinical diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HCV core antigen
Aptamer
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise-modified electrode
Optimization of analytical conditions and incubation time of HCV core antigen
Electrochemical response of the aptasensor to HCV core antigen

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study has been supported by the Ilam University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Nyquist plots recorded at different electrodes in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−; (a) GCE, (b) GQD/GCE, (c) Apt/GQD/GCE, (d) BSA/Apt/GQD/GCE at the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–10 kHz.
The 1cycle and 50 cycles voltammetric responses of Apt/GQD/GCE at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
Dependences of peak currents on incubation time of aptasensor.

Calibration curve: EIS response of electrochemical aptasensor in 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with increasing HCV core antigen concentration from at (0, 10, 20, 30, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400 pg mL−1) and the inset is the equivalent circuit and calibration curve of the impedance aptasensor for detecting HCV core antigen.
The response peak intensity for 5 electrodes which modified under the same conditions.
Schematic representation of electrochemical nanoaptasensor for HCV core antigen detection.
HCV core antigen detection in serum samples with developed aptasensor.
Comparison of performance of the HCV core antigen nanoaptasensor proposed in this work with some other methods.

head.ce:title: Ultra-sensitive aptasensor based on a GQD nanocomposite for detection of hepatitis C virus core antigen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Ilam, Ilam, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Islamic Azad University, Khorramabad Branch, Khorramabad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, by using the aptamer proximity binding assay strategy, a novel electrochemical aptasensor is described for ultrasensitive detection of hepatitis C virus (HCV) core antigen. The immobilization surface is prepared by the modification of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a graphene quantum dots (GQD). GQD were introduced as a novel and suitable substrate for aptamers through π-π stacking interactions, the richness of hydrophilic edges as well as hydrophobic plane in GQD which enhances the aptamer absorption on the electrode surface. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) were performed at each stage of the chemical modification process to confirm the resulting surface changes. EIS technique was used as an efficient alternative detection system for HCV core antigen measurement with detection limit 3.3 pg mL−1 and two linear concentration range 10–70 pg mL−1 and 70–400 pg mL−1. Moreover, the fabricated aptasensor could accurately detect HCV core antigen concentration in human serum samples. Such an aptasensor opens a rapid, selective and sensitive route for HCV core antigen detection and provides a promising strategy for potential applications in clinical diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: HCV mainly infects hepatocytes in the liver. It enters via the hepatic sinusoids associated with several lipoproteins. Therefore, it is one of the major causes of progressive liver disease. Infection with the HCV is a global health burden with approximately 170 million people chronically infected worldwide [1]. HCV is a single positive-strand RNA virus that is a member of the Hepacivirus genus in the Flaviviridae family. It has a genome about 9.6 kb in length that encodes a single long polyprotein which is processed into core, E1, E2 structural proteins and non-structural proteins consisting of the p7 ion channel, NS2, NS3, NS4A, NS4B, NS5A and NS5B by cellular and viral enzymes [2–4]. Interferon alpha (IFN-α) -based therapy is the current treatment for patients with chronic hepatitis C, many patients do not response to the therapy [5–7]. Development of safe, effective and well-tolerated drugs against HCV infection has been a strong motivation in academy and clinical. Core-specific aptamers may hold promise for the development as early diagnostic reagents for HCV infection and potential therapeutic agents for hepatitis C patients. We selected DNA aptamers [8] that can bind to the HCV core antigen with high affinity and specificity.

Aptamers are short, single-stranded oligonucleotides that are isolated by selective evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) approach [9]. Numerous high-affinity and highly specific aptamers have been generated against a wide variety of target molecules including small organics, peptides, proteins, and even complex-target such as whole cell [10–12]. Aptamers have become increasingly important molecular tools for diagnostics and therapeutics [13,14]. Aptamers are analogous to antibodies but possess many advantages over antibody as therapeutic reagents, including low cost, no immunogenicity, greater stability, easy synthesis and modification, smaller molecular weight [15,16].

GQDs are zero-dimension graphene material in the form of one, two or more layers all less than 10 nm thick and 100 nm in lateral size; also, they usually contain functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, epoxide) at their edges that can act as reaction sites [17]. Hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at their edges, involved absorption of the Apts onto the GQDs surface through non-covalent interactions of the electrostatic, hydrogen-bonding and π–π stacking types [18]. GQDs have been defined as low toxic, biocompatible NPs; however, as a consequence of their potential environmental release page and their easy dispersion. Due to their high water solubility, GQDs could accumulate in aquatic ecosystems and the human body (organs and tissues). Due to the aqueous nature of the biological environment, altering the GQDs surface properties from hydrophobic to hydrophilic becomes an essential step for GQDs to be useful in biological applications [19], GQDs with small size and large surface and their dimensional similarities with biological macro molecules can also form conjugate with them [20–22], Thus, GQDs have raised as attractive tools for research in various fields for designing sensors and biosensors (probes, electrochemical sensors and hybrid sensors), such as immunosensors and aptasensors [23–26].

Herein, we developed an EIS strategy to employ GQDs as the nanocomposite for ultrasensitive detection of HCV core antigen. In the proposed method, the Apt was immobilized onto the GQDs to the surface of the GCE through non-covalent interactions of the electrostatic, hydrogen-bonding and π–π stacking types. In this study, ferricyanide/ferrocyanide has been introduced as a redox probe in detection of HCV core antigen. To evaluate the utility of the ferricyanide/ferrocyanide as a probe for analytical application, it was used for the EIS detection of HCV core antigen. The findings indicate that this method had several distinct advantages, including low cost for preparation, good detection limit, simple and effective for HCV core antigen. To the best of our knowledge, an application of BSA/Apt/GQD/GCE and ferricyanide/ferrocyanide, a probe for the detection of HCV core antigen, has never been reported before.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.

The GCE was subsequently polished with 0.03 μm alumina powders on polishing cloth. The electrode was placed in mixture of water and ethanol at a ratio of 1:1 and bath ultrasonic cleaner was used in order to remove adsorbed particles. 10 μl GQD was placed on to the surface of a GCE and drying under room temperature, then a GQD modified GCE was fabricated. Afterwards, the GCE modified with GQD was washed with PB solution and immersed in 3 μL PB solution, containing aptamer (10 μM) that was prepared overnight to immobilize aptamer molecules on the surface of GQD by chemisorption between GQD and amine groups of aptamer. Abundance of carboxyl groups of the GQDs enabled strong adsorption of aptamer to the surface of GQDs through π-π stacking interactions [27]. The electrode was incubated in 1% BSA solution for about 1 h to block possible remaining active sites of the GQD and to avoid nonspecific adsorptions. After each step, the modified electrode surface was washed with deionized water in order to remove the physically and weakly adsorbed substrates, and finally, aptasensor was obtained. The prepared aptasensor was stored at 4 °C when not in use. By incubation of different antigen concentrations, the Apt/antigen complex was formed on the modified electrode surface. In the presence of antigen, inhabitation of the electron transfer of redox probe was significantly increased, which confirmed the formation of the Apt/antigen complex with a strong interaction between them led to more stearic effect of the molecules onto the modified electrode surface. Changes of the electrochemical signal were monitored by EIS (Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) as the measuring technique. The fabrication process, stability of the proposed nanoaptasensor was studied by CV, DPV and EIS as the effective electrochemical techniques. Scheme 1 shows the schematic representation for the fabrication of the HCV core antigen nanoaptasensor.
This section is presented in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the stepwise-modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: EIS is an effective tool for probing the features of the modified electrode surface. The change in the values of charge transfer resistance (Rct) for different modified electrodes was chosen to study the surface properties during the fabrication process of the proposed nanoaptasensor. To monitor modifications of the GCE surface properties step by step, CV (Fig. S3), DPV (Fig. S4) and EIS (Fig. 1 ) plots of different electrodes were recorded at a frequency range from 0.1 to 100 kHz in a solution of 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, as a redox probe, and 0.1 M KCl (at a ratio of 1:1) displays EIS plots, obtained at a potential of 0.25 V, for bare GCE (a) GQD/GCE (b), Apt/GQD/GCE (c), BSA/Apt/GQD/GCE (d). Randles equivalent circuit RS (Cdl Rct) was used to fit the experimental data. Rs is the solution resistance, Rct is the electron-transfer resistance, Cdl is the double layer capacitance (Inner Fig. 1).

The curves presents the electrochemical impedance of the bare GCE (charge transfer resistance (Rct = 153.46 Ω). The impedance spectrum of the GCE (Fig. 1a) the low Rct on the bare GCE indicated the fast electron transfer rate between CCE and [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−. The diameter of the semicircle for GQD/GCE (Fig. 1b) was larger than that of bare GCE, which it indicating that the GQD on the surface of GCE decreased the electron transfer rat between the redox probe of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−, and the electrode surface. Plenty of polar and negatively charged oxygen function in a group such as–COOH prevent [Fe (CN)6]3−/4−, from penetrating the electrode/solution interference. Thus, the presence of GQDs led to a greater repulsion of the electrode surface with redox anions and a greater prohibition in the electron transfer, which increased the Rct value to 2070 Ω, subsequently, when the HCV core protein specific aptmer was placed on the surface of GQD/GCE Rct increased dramatically (Rct = 11109 Ω), which indicates that the aptmer had been immobilized on the electrode surface and further prevented the redox probe to the electrode surface. This can be attributed to the repulsion between the negative charges of the phosphate groups in the Apt and redox probe anions (Fig. 1c). After incubating of aptasensor in BSA solution Rct increased further more in the same way; (Fig. 1d), Rct = 31786). Similar behavior was observed from the CVs and DPVs on the modified electrodes in the presence of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− couple, confirming the EIS results. The stability of the redox system was also investigated by recording the consequent cyclic voltammograms of modified electrode. The results indicate that only a 3% decrease in peak current of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox system was observed after 50 repetitive cycles (Fig. 2 ), which it indicating the high stability of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox system. In addition, the modified electrode response was 96% of its initial activity after it had been kept at 4 °C for 10 days. This indicates that the storage stability of the modified electrode was very good.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of analytical conditions and incubation time of HCV core antigen
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental conditions are important parameters for a high electrochemical response, which strongly influence the analytical performance. Because the Apt is a biological molecular, chemical environment must be such that the Apt will not be destroyed. Therefore, a neutral environment is essential. So, all of the experiments were carried out in a PB solution with pH of 7.4. This value is in accordance with the literature [11–13,30,31]. The incubation time is an important parameter for complex formation between aptamer and antigen. The effect of incubation time on the aptasensor is shown in Fig. 3 , the change of current response with the increase in incubation time in core antigen solution. With increasing incubation time from 5 min to 60 min, the current signal of DPV as an aptasensor response increased. After 40 min, no further significant increases occurred when increasing the incubation time. Thus, 40 min was chosen as the optimum incubation time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical response of the aptasensor to HCV core antigen
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high ability of EIS technique for probing the inter facial properties at the electrode surface and the possibility of performing label-free detections [32], EIS measurement was also applied as an efficient alternative detection system to further confirm the analytical performance of the proposed system. Fig. 4 displays the Nyquist plots obtained at the BSA/Apt/GQD/GCE electrode after being incubated with different concentrations of target core antigen in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. As shown in this figure, the Rct value increased with increasing the concentration of HCV core antigen that is due to the hindrance of electron transfer process of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- at electrode surface. The Rct was proportional to the HCV core antigen concentration at two concentration ranges, for lower concentration range (10–70 pg mL−1), the linear equation could be depicted as ΔRct = 0.5997 [antigen] (pg mL−1)+35.28 and the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.97, while for higher concentration of the HCV core antigen (70–400 pg mL−1) the corresponding regression equation was ΔRct = 0.0503 [antigen] (pg mL−1) +71.493 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.999, also a detection limit (LOD) of 3.3 pg mL−1 (based on S/N = 3). This clearly indicates that the proposed system can also be used as an efficient ultrasensitive impedimetric aptasensor for HCV core antigen detection.

The stability of the developed nanoaptasensor was investigated by the CV techniques. The CVs related to the modified electrode, revealed that its voltammetric behavior was very stable and the modified electrode retained about 97% of its initial response, after 50 repetitive cycles. Additionally, the storage stability of the modified electrode was very good as the electrode was found to 96% its initial activity for 10 days when kept at 4 °C. In addition, the modified electrode response was 85% of its initial activity after it had been kept at 4 °C for 14 days. Furthermore reproducibility of the electrodes was investigated. The peak current response of the modified electrode was determined with 5 electrodes which produced under the same conditions. The response peak intensity showed a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.3% confirming that the results are reproducible (Fig. 5 ).

In order to estimate the applicability of the developed aptasensor as a sensing method for real sample analysis, specific amounts of HCV core antigen in to human serum samples was spiked. The blood serum of a healthy man were ordered from a local clinical laboratory and subjected to ultrafiltration by loading into a centrifugal filtration tube at 3000 rpm (30 min). Afterwards, the serum samples were diluted 50 times with PB solution (0.1 M). The DPV technique and the standard addition method were used for HCV core antigen detection and for determining the recoveries. We spiked diluted serum samples with different concentrations of antigen 35, 40, 50 and 150 pg/mL. The obtained recoveries for spiked antigen concentrations was in the range of 99–109%. The reported results in Table 1 indicate that fabricated aptasensor in this study can be used for quantitative detection of HCV core antigen in real samples.

A simple electrochemical aptasensor was firstly developed for HCV core antigen based on the high specificity and affinity of the aptamer. The aptasensor was constructed by utilizing the GQD in order to immobilize aptamer on the GCE. GQD due to their special size and surface effect are among the most promising nanocrystals with unique chemical and electrochemical properties. The results suggested that the aptasensor can be used for detection of HCV core antigen at low concentration range pg mL−1. The application of the proposed aptasensor for HCV core antigen detection with good precision and accuracy in human serum as real sample was also evaluated. As shown in Table 2 , several studies have revealed accurate and sensitive diagnosis of HCV in blood samples but a few of them uses HCV core antigen levels rather than anti-HCV and HCV RNA. Despite their accuracy and reliability, all these approaches have certain practical disadvantages such as complexity, time consuming, high cost and cumbersome application. Therefore, it deems necessary to develop a simple, rapid and economical approach for ultrasensitive and selective measurement of HCV core antigen. Performance of the developed nanoaptasensor was superior to that of the other methods reported in the related literature, especially in comparison to the reported LODs. Furthermore, the simple fabrication process is major advantage in comparison to other methods. This electrochemical aptasensor was easy to produce, and might prove promising for applications in biological or clinical target analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study has been supported by the Ilam University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ab.2017.07.016.
